The Email Service is a multi-tenant email delivery platform that provides a single, consistent API for sending emails regardless of the underlying email provider being used. The primary goal of the service is to remove all provider-specific logic from client applications and centralize email delivery, template management, auditing, security, and provider configuration within a dedicated service. Applications integrating with the service should never need to know whether email is being sent through Resend, SendGrid, Amazon SES, Mailgun, SMTP, or any future provider. Instead, they simply call the Email Service and allow the platform to determine the appropriate delivery mechanism based on tenant configuration.

The service is designed to operate within a broader ecosystem of shared services. Tenant configuration, user information, API key validation, permissions, and secrets are all managed externally by the Auth/Tenant Service. The Email Service relies on this service to determine how email should be sent for a particular tenant. Every request to the Email Service includes an X-Tenant-ID, X-APIKEY, and X-Hostname header. These values identify the originating application, validate permissions, and determine which tenant configuration should be used during processing.

Each tenant can independently configure its email provider, sender details, delivery mode, and provider-specific settings. This allows one tenant to use Resend with synchronous delivery while another tenant uses Amazon SES with asynchronous queued delivery, all while consuming the same API endpoints. The Email Service retrieves the required configuration from the Auth/Tenant Service and uses provider-specific secrets that are also managed externally. Secrets are never stored within the Email Service itself. Instead, they are requested dynamically through the tenant service whenever they are required.

The service exposes a general-purpose email sending endpoint and a template-based email sending endpoint. The general sending endpoint allows applications to construct and submit complete emails directly. The template endpoint allows applications to reference a predefined template using a unique template code. Template codes are unique only within a tenant, allowing different tenants to define their own versions of common templates such as password-reset, welcome, invoice, or invitation. When a template-based email is sent, the Email Service retrieves the template associated with the supplied tenant and template code, merges the provided parameters into the template, generates the final email content, and submits the email through the configured provider.

Templates are stored within the Email Service database and consist of a subject template and an HTML body template. Template rendering is performed using parameter substitution, where values supplied in the request body replace placeholders contained within the template. This allows applications to standardize email content while still supplying dynamic information such as names, links, account details, reference numbers, dates, and other contextual information. Templates are intended to be managed centrally, allowing content changes to be made without requiring application deployments.

Recipients may be specified either as direct email addresses or as user identifiers. When a user identifier is supplied, the Email Service communicates with the Auth/Tenant Service to retrieve the user's current email address and profile information. This approach allows applications to send emails to users without needing to store or manage email addresses themselves. Sending directly to arbitrary email addresses is considered a higher privilege operation and requires additional permissions to be granted to the API key being used. This prevents applications from unintentionally becoming open email relays and ensures that external email delivery remains a deliberate capability.

The Email Service supports both attachments and embedded images. Files may be supplied directly as part of the request or referenced through existing platform services. Attachments can be uploaded directly or referenced using file codes managed by the Files Service. When a file code is provided, the Email Service retrieves the file content before delivery. Similarly, images can either be supplied directly or referenced using image codes managed by the Imaginary Service. This allows applications to reuse existing assets without repeatedly transmitting large files while still supporting scenarios where files must be generated dynamically at runtime.

The service provides a provider abstraction layer that isolates application logic from email vendor implementations. Internally, each provider implements a common interface that receives a normalized email object and is responsible for transforming that object into the provider-specific format required by the external API. This architecture allows new providers to be introduced without affecting clients or requiring changes to the public API. It also enables tenant-level provider selection and simplifies future migrations between providers.

Email delivery behavior is controlled through tenant configuration. Some tenants may require immediate confirmation that an email has been accepted by the provider, while others may prefer asynchronous processing for improved performance and resilience. For tenants configured for synchronous delivery, the Email Service performs all validation, template rendering, file retrieval, recipient resolution, and provider communication during the request lifecycle. The response is returned only after the provider has accepted or rejected the email. For tenants configured for asynchronous delivery, the Email Service validates and stores the email before placing it into a processing queue. A background worker then performs delivery at a later stage. This approach allows each tenant to select the most appropriate balance between responsiveness and reliability without requiring different APIs.

Regardless of delivery mode, the Email Service maintains a comprehensive audit trail of email activity. Every email processed by the platform is recorded within the service database along with the originating tenant, request details, recipients, provider information, delivery status, timestamps, and any provider-specific response information. This ensures that administrators can review historical email activity, investigate failures, confirm successful delivery attempts, and troubleshoot integration issues. The audit trail serves as both an operational monitoring tool and a compliance record of communication activity.

The service includes a logging endpoint that allows authorized systems to retrieve email history for a tenant. Logs can be used to view sent emails, identify delivery failures, inspect provider responses, and confirm recipient information. The logging functionality is intended to provide visibility into email activity without requiring administrators to access provider-specific dashboards. Over time, this capability may be expanded to include filtering, searching, delivery analytics, and reporting features.

Security is a core aspect of the platform design. Every request is validated against the tenant and API key supplied in the request headers. The Email Service never stores provider credentials locally and instead retrieves secrets from the tenant management platform when required. Recipient resolution is performed through trusted internal services, and direct email delivery permissions are enforced through API key capabilities. Tenant isolation is maintained throughout the system to ensure that templates, logs, configurations, and email activity belonging to one tenant can never be accessed by another tenant.

The resulting platform provides a centralized, provider-independent email delivery solution that standardizes email operations across all applications in the ecosystem. It enables applications to send transactional and operational emails through a simple API while delegating template management, provider integration, recipient resolution, auditing, security, and delivery configuration to a dedicated service. This approach reduces duplication across applications, simplifies provider migrations, and creates a consistent, maintainable email infrastructure that can evolve as new providers, features, and delivery requirements emerge.
